task: I need to send a brief reply to a company about a notice I received, and I want to make sure I don’t include anything unnecessary. Can you help me shape it so it covers the point without opening the door to extra back-and-forth?

prefs.explanation: She is unlikely to appreciate hedging or soft-pedaling when she asks for help. Her life pattern is to cut through confusion quickly and verify what matters, so direct answers fit her style. Overly delicate wording would probably feel evasive or wasteful to her.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: She tends to act on practical information and would likely prefer the core point first. Long-winded responses would feel like clutter unless she specifically asks for detail. She seems to value efficiency in communication the same way she values efficiency in paperwork and routines.

prefs.short: Likes organized checklists and clear steps
prefs.explanation: She responds best to information that is easy to verify and follow in order. A structured format would match her record-keeping habits and reduce the chance of missing something important. Loose, rambling prose would be less useful to her than a clean list or step-by-step outline.

She is not looking for warmth that feels performative or sentimental. A steady, neutral tone would likely feel more trustworthy because it avoids trying to manipulate her mood. She probably dislikes being treated as fragile or emotionally steered.

She is very protective of her personal space and information, so she would likely want interactions that do not pry. She would probably be uncomfortable with unnecessary requests for private details or casual data-sharing. A restrained approach that asks only for what is needed would fit her well.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it short and specific:

> I dispute the amount in your notice. Please send the account number, the date the charge was posted, a full itemized breakdown, and the basis for each charge. Please also hold any collection or late-action activity until the documentation is provided. Please respond in writing.

If you want, I can make it:
- firmer
- more neutral
- even shorter

per_pref.reason: Very direct and blunt: "I dispute the amount in your notice" and "Please send..." with no hedging.
Concise and to the point, with a short draft and only a brief offer to adjust it further.
Uses a clear structured request list: account number, date, itemized breakdown, basis for each charge, hold activity, respond in writing.
Mostly matter-of-fact and neutral; phrases like "I dispute the amount" and the plain request list avoid emotional language.
It does avoid unnecessary detail in the draft itself, but it still asks for several pieces of information and offers follow-up options, so it is only partially minimal.

turns.agent_response:
That’s a good version. I’d use it exactly like this:

> I dispute this notice. Send the account number, date posted, and an itemized breakdown with the basis for each charge. Put any collection or late-action on hold until you provide it. Reply in writing only.

If you want, I can make it even shorter for email subject/body format.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and no-nonsense: "I dispute this notice" and "Reply in writing only" get straight to the point without hedging.
Concise and efficient; it gives a ready-to-send reply in a few sentences and even offers to make it "even shorter."
Uses a clear, ordered request list: "account number, date posted, and an itemized breakdown..." followed by a direct instruction.
Matter-of-fact and neutral throughout; no sentimental or overly warm language, just practical wording.
Minimizes disclosure by asking only for necessary details and explicitly limits contact with "Reply in writing only."
